speaker: interviewer
text: It's upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, upstairs is only one person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's her television. It's her television.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm hearing it a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we ask them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm going to ask her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you ask her to turn it down? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I am hearing just a little bit of an aside.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sort of sounds like kids playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the TV, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I have you scoot over just a little bit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which way? To the right or the left?

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards the right. She's getting more comfortable and I had a light stand that's right on the edge of my frame there.

words.text: and I had a light stand

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is this OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to scoot your chair over just a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me pick up my water before we do that. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's have you go over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can sneak over a little bit, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. She's got a gorgeous face, doesn't she.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's just shin-- This is Cleveland, Ohio, April 16th with Betty Gold. Tape number two. And we're speeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I'm going to ask you, when Hitler came to power, what do you remember? What were the first signs of anti-Semitism that you saw as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my-- witnessing, the first time that I witnessed was a bunch of Polish men attacking a bunch of Jewish boys, including my brother-- right in front of my house in the garden that he made and planted in front of our house. And my brother was older at that time, and he was very involved in Betar and some other organizations as a youngster. And I guess they knew of him. And it was like a Sunday. On the way back from church, they had to pass our town, our shtetl, the Gentile people. And on the way back from church, they attacked these boys. My brother was one of them. He was pretty beaten up, black and blue, real, real bad. And everyone started to run and close their doors and hide inside because they were afraid of their children or themselves getting beaten up. And after that my brother was forced to leave the country because they threatened to kill him if they catch him again just because he's Jewish and because he's active in-- he's a Zionist. Well, he left for Argentina and eventually he, unfortunately, he died there. Got-- was electrocuted taking a bath. He escaped Hitler, but he got killed in Argentina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Shimon, my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, but I thought, what about your-- OK. But there were-- how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm very sorry. I got off track. That was my uncle, my father's brother. I beg your pardon. That was my father's brother. My brother was there also. He got beat up by accident, but it was my uncle, my father's youngest brother, that got attacked. He was very active. He was a Zionist. And they were after him. My brother just happened to be there by coincidence in his garden. And they attacked my brother as well as they were after my uncle. And my uncle was the one who was forced to leave the country. He was sent away to Argentina, and eventually he was-- he electrocuted himself taking a bath in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's-- I saw that with my own eyes. And I was screaming. I was hysterical. And I was crying. And I also witnessed many, many times where-- mostly it would take place when, on Sundays, when they would go to or from church. And they had to pass our shtetl. And that's when they, they would call us dirty Jew, and call us names, and, and hit us, and we tried to stay out of their way.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't that bad yet. But with the child's eyes, whatever I saw already had an effect on me. I realized that I'm Jewish. And I realized that they don't like us. And I had to be careful and stay out of the way when they were walking and so forth. And most of these things happened on Sundays. Although my father had some friends because he supplied him with their boots and shoes and everything, but behind his back they still were anti-Semitic. And they were always threatening that, that we'll never get out of the shtetl alive. In one moment they would do business and pat my father on the back, and the next moment they would threaten him that we're dirty Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew our place, and we just got out of the way and behaved ourselves accordingly. And a lot of young people did leave in those days. They went to Palestine. A lot of them went to South America, and so forth. So I do remember anti-Semitism. Do you remember-- what can you tell me about-- if at all, because you were such a small child-- when Hitler came to power in Germany, do you remember overhearing anything on the radio? You said you got the radio. Do you remember people talking? Or did it have any effect on you? Were you so far east that it didn't have any effect on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a great effect on me. I was personally affected because I remember 1939 the Russians came and took over our shtetl. And then when the war broke out in 1939, they were going to take my father in the army. And my father didn't want to go in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a secret, my dad and I. There was a certain flower-- they were dandelions, I think. And he told me that I should go out and pick dandelions. And we would go in the attic above in our house, and he put on dandelions on his arm and wrap it around. And eventually it caused tremendous sores in his arm-- I mean really bad. I think they would amputate it here. But he did it to stay out of the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm the only one who knew about it-- not my brothers, nobody. And my dad and I would hide in the attic, and I would do the applications every day until it got bad enough so he would-- they would not take him to the army. And it just so happened by the time he was-- the arm was ripe and ready, the war was over. And then, of course, Hitler took over. And the first thing I remember-- and this is really a true story. I was a young kid and I remember getting up in the morning and I told my father that-- I was very close with my dad. I always told him stories. And I told him I dreamt there was a war. Because people were talking anyway, and I think what you hear, so I dreamt about it. And I says, there's a war. And that same day on the radio they announced that the war broke out. And the first-- the first thing that happened is we didn't have industry to bomb or anything like that. But I remember the planes flying over the shtetl, and the bombs were dropping out of nowhere. I mean, they came out and everybody started running into the woods. We were-- the Radziwill woods were right in back of our shtetl all over. And so everybody was running away to the woods, or hiding in the gardens, or just lying down, wherever they could. And I don't know how many bombs were dropping, but it was just-- I've never heard anything like this in my life. Noises, and the sound of the bombs, and the screams, and the hysterics of the mothers, and babies, and children. And I was hiding next door in the garden, and then I saw a bomb drop and kill my goat, my brother's goat, actually. And that was-- that was absolutely the worst thing I ever witnessed in my entire life. And I thought that was-- that was a Holocaust to me. And it was my brother's goat and he was beside himself. And it destroyed our garden, and it destroyed a few homes, and a few people got-- I don't remember if anyone got killed, but I remember some injuries. And they were flying very, very low, like on top of the roof. I mean, I was-- we could see the soldiers, the Nazis, inside the plane when we looked up. That's how low they were flying because we didn't have any tall buildings, and, you know, it was just flat homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was devastating. I mean it was just devastating-- mothers, and children, and babies, and cries. What did they bomb for? I mean, obviously they were just after the civilians because there was nothing to bomb except the homes. Why would they want to destroy the homes at that time? They weren't killing the Jews yet, in the beginning. So that subsided after a while, and everyone went back to their grief and their home and put things in order again. And eventually the Nazis came, took over our town. And while we were there, we already saw refugees coming from Warsaw and from other parts, from Poland. They were running away already from the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, this is unbelievable. My parents, my aunts, my uncles, my grandparents, they were planting gardens for next year. And these refugees are running through the gardens, running through the woods, coming into our shtetl to hide out, and they didn't even stop planting the seeds. They said, ah they must have done something wrong. They're running away because they probably did something wrong and they're afraid the Nazis will get them. And they just continued like they're going to eat them next spring, or something, like the fruits going to be there, and vegetables.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I look back, I just-- I guess people could never believe that this is going to happen to us too. They didn't listen. They didn't listen. Or if they listened, they didn't believe it. It was just beyond comprehension what people told us. And I was young and a little child, I said, well, I said, mom, if that's happening to them, why don't we run too someplace? Why are we planting the seeds? They are going to throw the bombs. We won't have it anyway. I don't know what the answer was, but I remember those refugees running into our town, and I remember my family's planting seeds. And I, myself, you know, as a young child, was wondering if we ever are going to enjoy the fruits of their labor. Why did they do it? What for? They didn't believe it. They didn't believe what's going on. We didn't even know yet. So that was a very interesting observation and experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a lot of refugees that came. And people shared their homes with them. We took them in. And it was tough. It was very tough at that time. And I think reality started to set in. And people already heard from the refugees eventually as time went on of what's happening in Warsaw, and in Lubin and all the other big cities. And people started to either-- some of them left for Russia, you know went across. It was still-- you were still able to do that. And some of them already prepared themselves in the event that they're going to come to us where to hide, or what to do. My father and a cousin of his decided-- we had like a-- well, it wasn't like a garage. We stored wood in there, and products, and food, and whatnot. It was like a big wooden garage in the back of our home. And it was long, narrow. And my father decided with his cousin that they're going to build a wall there, a wooden wall. In case the Nazi's come, we'll just go in the wall and hide, a secret wall. And they built it. Nobody knew. It was done secretly at night. And then like they took away everybody's businesses and gold and jewelry, or whatever each one had. And they used a man. And they took all the man away to work in the factories or whatever they delegated they did in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean were they-- wait, I want to just clarify this a bit. Were they taken into forced labor camps where they went every day by the train, or by something, is that what you're talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a very small shtetl so transportation wasn't necessary. They were just taken to clean roads, to work in the-- they needed the leather goods for the army, I guess, for the soldiers. So they, and we had leather goods factories, like small shops. And everybody had to work there, all the men. If one was a peddler, if one was something else, everybody had to work in the leather factories. And after that-- and they continued like that. And, of course, everything was very sparse. We didn't have just, a little bit of whatever we had left in the gardens to live on. And, and then they took everybody. They started take some men away from out of the shtetl. And they said they're taking them to work, in this town, and that town. We didn't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the men started to hide. My father was hidden in that wall. And when they came to us and they asked where my father, and my older brother, we said, I don't know. He's someplace in Stutt. You know, he's working someplace. We don't know. They took him yesterday. He went to work. He hid out in that wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, it was like a hot summer day, I remember. And they surrounded the shtetl, and they took everybody out of their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the summer of 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or, 1940, rather. In the summer of 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember it was a summer day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that first winter-- just to back up. I'm sorry to interrupt you, but that first winter you kind of continued your lives on some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some fashion, whatever was possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you have food, and how did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we prepared from the gardens, you know, so we stored for the winter and so forth, and whatever rations, little bit they gave us. Then they surrounded the town, and they took everybody into a ghetto. They took them up town. There were, I don't know, about 10 houses or something. And everybody had to leave their home. And you were allowed to take whatever you can carry. So we all schlepped whatever we could carry, and we went to the ghetto. In the meantime, my father had put in the wall some flour, and some-- not flour, but they had to be ready to eat stuff. And I don't remember exactly what, but he put some breads, and-- I don't know what the food was, but he prepared food in case we have to hide in the wall. So there was food, maybe for a couple weeks, or a week. And we left everything in the wall, and we all went to the ghetto. There was my-- my immediate family was my father, my mother, my brother, and my grandmother who lived with us at the time. My father's mother lived with us at that time. So it was my grandmother, my parents, and my two brothers, and I. And my mother's mother was across the street, and she was going to go with us, but she didn't want to go to the ghetto. So she hid. And before we left we saw her, they took her out and shot her. This little lady, this sweet little grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do-- what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just pulled her out of there and shot her because they got mad at her for hiding. Why she didn't go with everybody in the ghetto. So we took whatever we could, and we walked with the crow. And I don't know, maybe 1,000 people from where we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to the ghetto. And once we got to the ghetto my father and mother, two of my-- my cousin who built the wall, his wife and their three children, my two brothers, that's it, they went back because you were allowed to make, like, two trips, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went back but they never-- and they left me with my grandmother in the ghetto, my father's mother, in the ghetto to watch the belongings. And I sat there with her, and I realized that nobody came back from my immediate family. Right away, it dawned on me they must be in the wall. And I got scared. And I got angry why they left me. And they were hiding. And my cousin and his wife and three children were there. And they were little kids, younger than me. One baby was younger than me. And I was very upset. And I was so torn. My grandmother sitting there with the [NON-ENGLISH] with her belongings, and I'm sitting next to her and all the other Jews. But I wanted to live, and I didn't know what to do. She couldn't go back, and I left her. That was a very tough day of my life, probably the worst. And I ran back. I knew they had to be in the wall. And as I was going people were coming to the ghetto, and I was going the opposite way. And you couldn't go through the back. You just had to go on the main street, was all. And in order for me to get out-- the crowd was coming towards me, and with, you know, the Nazis, and the shotguns in back of them. And I saw my cousins, and my aunts, and my uncles coming and carrying the babies and everybody. And it was Germans with guns and Ukrainians, too. And I went-- one of the Germans was busy looking up, and I went through his legs at one point. Under his legs I crawled. He didn't see me. He happened to be standing that way. And I passed by, and I pushed myself. I was a little girl. Maybe they didn't see me. They were like cattle. And I approached my house and I saw there were soldiers far away. And I crawled on the ground like there was a drive and I just crawled between the twigs and the bushes. And I got to the wall, and they wouldn't let me in. They were afraid that the Nazis, that the Nazis got me and they put me up to find out where the rest of the family is and that I would lead them to the family. So they wouldn't let me in, and they didn't answer. I finally started crying and said, there's nobody with me. There's nobody with me. I'm just alone. I'm just alone. And I convinced them to open the secret door and they let me in. That was the last time we saw, at that time, the rest of the family. That night-- that night it was already late in the evening by then. We were 17 people in that wall, but three small children. And my cousin's youngest baby was crying. And the mother choked her because she wanted to save the other two little boys. They were older. They understood better. And I peeked out through the cracks of the wood. There was like pine. And nobody was allowed to cough, or cry, or breathe, actually, because Nazis were all over the place. And they were holding our mouths. And if you wanted to sneeze, or something, they were choking us and putting, you know, stuff in our mouths not to sneeze, not to cough, not to talk. And I looked out at one point in the cracks. It was still daylight and they had a truck full of babies-- an open truck, not closed, just two sides and the back. And there was two soldiers, or three-- two that I saw through the crack. And they just kept-- what they did is they pick those babies by their arms, by their legs, as they were walking. They were still leading them to the ghetto and throwing them on the truck. And the mothers were reaching and screaming and crying. And I saw that and I just, I couldn't understand it. What it was all about. I thought it wasn't for real. Like I saw my little cousins baby, or my little cousin thrown on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do? I mean, how does a person control themselves? How does a child control and not scream and cry? We knew we had to be quiet and keep our mouth shut. And you watch it, and you don't do anything about it, that you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it's-- I don't know what we could have done. Except that if I would run out they'd put me in the truck, too. Now, the feeling that I had, I can't explain to this day. I just keep thinking it's a dream, that I saw it in the movie, that it really didn't happen. But I saw it with my own eyes. I will never forget this, never. I know those faces. I know those babies. They were familiar to me. I've seen them every single day in my life. And I thought, gee, maybe they'll put them on the truck and just take them to the ghetto. They don't want them to walk, or they don't want-- they don't have the patience to wait till the babies will be able to get there, so they took them in a truck. And maybe that's how they're transporting them just to the ghetto. And then they'll give them to the parents. Not so, no such luck. And they took, that night we heard a lot of shooting. While we were in the wall, it was raining. We heard a lot of shooting. What they did, this I did not see, but we heard the shooting. Which was maybe a mile away from us, so you heard it real loud. Of course, we found out from survivor, afterwards, that ran away from the shooting, that they made the rabbi, and the parents, the caves, the big caves, three caves they had. And they dug the caves and then the Germans shot the people, and the babies, and children. They put them all in three caves. And whoever-- and they picked strong men, a lot of men, they let them live yet, and a lot of women.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some ran. They didn't care. They want to get killed while they are running instead of going to the graves. So they ran away to the woods. Some got shot down on the way. Some didn't. That was what they told us afterwards, those who survived until the next killing. Tell us, how did you get away? And they told us, this is how. They got, they ran away to the woods, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they told us that they dug the graves, and they buried all the people in town. And, you know, and they told us that a lot of people did run away to the woods. We found this out, of course, months later. And what we did in the middle of the night when it was raining-- we were so grateful it was raining-- like 24 hours later, the next night. We crawled out of the wall because it was quiet, and we had a hard time getting out of the wall for the first 24 hours because they picked our house to store the people's gold, and clothes, certain valuables. We had a very nice house with big rooms. I don't know why they picked our house. So there were so many soldiers there working and packing stuff into the house that we couldn't escape. They would see us. So we had to wait till it cleared or subsided. So it took about 24-- two nights. And then it was raining and there was a big garden next to that wall to that garage, or whatever you want to call it, barn. And we climbed out at night, and it was raining so we were hopeful that nobody will hear us. And we crawled out. And we got into the garden. Now, the garden had-- I don't know, what would you call these, trenches between for the water to drain. So we got in the trenches. They were long, maybe a half a mile. We got into the trenches in the rain. So it was noisy because the leaves were making noise. And we crawled-- all of us that were in the wall, we crawled into the woods. We made it to the Radziwill woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me? The woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like Prince Radziwill? That belonged to them. That was their woods. They were our neighbors. They lived, of course, a little bit nicer than we did. But they were our neighbors. And we ended up in the woods. And we survived on-- we weren't prepared where to hide and what to do, so we had to stay where there was a lot of trees. And thank god it was summer so the leaves were still-- trees were full and blooming, and so it was easier to hide. The visibility, you know, wasn't good. So we stayed in the woods. And while we were in the woods, then, my cousin, my father's cousin, and my dad, and there were a couple more men, we decided-- they decided that they will build caves in different spots of the woods, in different areas, maybe three miles apart, four miles apart, to prepare for the winter. And so if they find us, or spot us in one place, we'll have someplace else to run to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they dug about nine caves over a few months. And they covered them up with sticks, and wood, and trees. And they left-- they would leave a very small entrance where you could just crawl in. So the top looked like it was soil. You know, you couldn't tell. It was leaves and twigs. There was the roof of the cave. So was it like an underground bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: An underground bunker. A cave, right, with a small entrance that was covered up with leaves, and trees, and so forth, little trees, you know. And we started to live in those. While it was warm we stayed outside, and we stayed in a swamp at one time because we figured while it's summer, we can survive outdoors. So there was a big, big swamp not far from us, very thick trees and close to each other. But the water was like up to here, up to your neck. And we decided to go in that swamp. And my dad and the other men built a bridge, a great big-- with trees. I don't know how they cut them. I don't remember. Maybe they found trees lying on the ground in the woods and they pulled them in. And they built, like, a bridge. We lived on that bridge, day and night, with the snakes and the birds. There was a snake that became our pet, stayed with us all the time, never hurt us. And we would-- to survive we would go out at night. And mostly I, because my brother, they were afraid that he's circumcised and if they find him they'll realize that he's Jewish and force him to lead us to where we're hiding. So was most of the time-- in the beginning, we all went for food at night. We used to steal from gardens, and orchards, and-- I shouldn't have done that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'll shut the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We happen to be out of--
